results.scenario_name: Scenario 21 — Multi-Parcel Tracking Partial Delivery Detection
results.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Shipment and Parcel clearly distinguished as aggregate root and child entity with separate lifecycles. (2) Shipment status explicitly and repeatedly described as derived/projected from parcel states — called out as the 'core domain insight' in BR-4 with invariant justification. (3) At least 7 parcel-level states defined (CREATED, IN_TRANSIT, OUT_FOR_DELIVERY, DELIVERED, LOST, RETURNED_TO_SENDER, DISPUTE_UNDER_REVIEW). (4) Business invariants comprehensively cover partial delivery detection (BR-4, BR-6, BR-11), lost parcel escalation (BR-9), and parcel-level dispute/refund (BR-8, BR-10, BR-12). (5) 9 non-trivial edge cases surfaced including out-of-order delivery, lost-then-found, duplicate scans, concurrent updates, misdelivery, and SLA-based escalation. (6) 10 testable acceptance criteria provided in Given/When/Then format.

observations.text: The derivation rule (shipment status projected from parcel states) is treated as the central insight — explicitly stated in the ubiquitous language, BR-4, and multiple acceptance criteria. This is the most critical domain insight and Bird nails it.
BR-9 correctly prohibits LOST → DELIVERED transition and requires a new parcel entity for recovered items, preserving financial integrity of insurance/refund workflows.
Edge cases are genuinely non-trivial: concurrent parcel updates, dispute on in-transit parcel (correctly rejected), single-parcel shipment treated uniformly, and time-based SLA escalation.
Parcel-level dispute granularity is thoroughly covered with BR-8, BR-12, and AC-6 — disputes reference specific parcels and do not affect siblings.
Business impact analysis includes all relevant stakeholder groups with concrete operational consequences, not generic statements.
Distinguishes invariants from policy rules (BR-10 refund granularity marked as non-invariant since a VP could change it) — shows mature domain thinking.

The parcel state machine transitions are described via business rules but never rendered as an explicit state diagram or transition table showing all valid from→to pairs. AC-5 tests one invalid transition, but a full transition matrix would strengthen the analysis.
Time-based escalation thresholds are acknowledged as unknown but no default or recommendation is offered — Bird could have proposed a strawman SLA window for engineering to validate.

results.agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Logistics platform refactoring from shipment-level tracking (single tracking number, single status) to parcel-level tracking. The business driver is partial delivery detection — the ability to know that 3 of 5 parcels arrived, trigger appropriate billing/refund/escalation workflows, and let customers dispute at parcel granularity rather than all-or-nothing shipment level.","bounded_context":"Shipment Fulfillment & Tracking","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"

meta.notes: Preliminary scoring by Coach K. Human review pending via web app at localhost:3000.
